Time-Dependent Perturbation Theory: From Fermi's Golden Rule to Linear Response ​

📅 Date: 2026-03-31 | 🏷️ Tags: Quantum Mechanics, Condensed Matter, Statistical Mechanics | 📂 Category: Quantum Mechanics Notes

Introduction ​

Time-dependent perturbation theory addresses quantum systems where the Hamiltonian carries explicit time dependence:

H^(t)=H^0+V^(t)

Unlike stationary perturbation theory where V^ is static and we seek energy eigenstates, here we track the dynamical evolution of quantum states under time-varying influences. This framework underlies scattering theory and condensed matter response functions.

Part I: The Interaction Picture and Dyson Series ​

Interaction Picture Formulation ​

Separate the Hamiltonian into H^0 (solvable) and V^(t) (perturbation). States and operators in the interaction picture are defined via:

|ψI(t)⟩=eiH^0t/ℏ|ψS(t)⟩,O^I(t)=eiH^0t/ℏO^Se−iH^0t/ℏ

The Schrödinger equation becomes:

iℏddt|ψI(t)⟩=V^I(t)|ψI(t)⟩

where V^I(t)=eiH^0t/ℏV^(t)e−iH^0t/ℏ.

Time-Evolution Operator ​

Define U^(t,t0) such that |ψI(t)⟩=U^(t,t0)|ψI(t0)⟩. The evolution operator satisfies:

iℏdU^dt=V^I(t)U^,U^(t0,t0)=1^

Integrating iteratively yields the Dyson series:

U^(t,t0)=1^+∑n=1∞(−iℏ)n∫t0tdt1∫t0t1dt2⋯∫t0tn−1dtnV^I(t1)V^I(t2)⋯V^I(tn)

Part II: First-Order Transition Amplitudes ​

Transition Probability ​

Suppose the system starts in eigenstate |i⟩ of H^0 at t0=0. To first order in V^:

|ψI(t)⟩≈|i⟩+1iℏ∫0tV^I(t′)|i⟩dt′

The amplitude for transition to state |f⟩ (f≠i) is:

cf(1)(t)=⟨f|ψI(t)⟩=1iℏ∫0teiωfit′Vfi(t′)dt′

where ωfi=(Ef−Ei)/ℏ and Vfi(t)=⟨f|V^(t)|i⟩.

Harmonic Perturbation and Fermi's Golden Rule ​

For monochromatic driving V^(t)=V^e−iωt+V^†eiωt, the first term dominates near resonance ω≈ωfi (absorption). The transition probability to a continuum of final states with density ρ(Ef) gives the transition rate:

Wi→f=2πℏ|Vfi|2ρ(Ef)

This is Fermi's Golden Rule—the fundamental result for transitions between discrete and continuous spectra.

Part III: Scattering Theory and Propagating States ​

From Time-Dependent to Stationary Scattering ​

Scattering problems involve particles approaching from infinity, interacting via potential V^, and emerging to infinity. While the full problem is time-dependent, the S-matrix connects asymptotic states at t→±∞.

The first-order S-matrix element for transition |i⟩→|f⟩ under perturbation V^(t) is:

Sfi(1)=−iℏ∫−∞∞eiωfit⟨f|V^(t)|i⟩dt

For a time-independent potential, this becomes:

Sfi(1)=−2πiδ(Ef−Ei)⟨f|V^|i⟩

The energy-conserving delta function reflects that elastic scattering preserves energy.

The Lippmann-Schwinger Equation ​

Stationary scattering states |ψ(±)⟩ with energy E satisfy an integral equation derived from the Schrödinger equation. Starting from:

(E−H^0)|ψ⟩=V^|ψ⟩

Formally inverting (E−H^0) requires care due to the singularity at E=En. The resolution is the resolvent operator with appropriate boundary conditions:

G^0(±)(E)=1E−H^0±iϵ

The ±iϵ prescription (ϵ→0+) selects:

  • +iϵ: outgoing spherical waves (retarded Green's function)
  • −iϵ: incoming spherical waves (advanced Green's function)

The Lippmann-Schwinger equation is:

|ψ(±)⟩=|ϕ⟩+G^0(±)(E)V^|ψ(±)⟩

where |ϕ⟩ is the incident plane wave satisfying (E−H^0)|ϕ⟩=0.

Born Approximation ​

Iterating the Lippmann-Schwinger equation generates the Born series:

|ψ(+)⟩=|ϕ⟩+G^0(+)V^|ϕ⟩+G^0(+)V^G^0(+)V^|ϕ⟩+⋯

The first-order Born approximation keeps only the single-scattering term:

|ψ(+)⟩≈|ϕ⟩+1E−H^0+iϵV^|ϕ⟩

The scattering amplitude f(k′,k) relates to the transition matrix element:

f(k′,k)=−m2πℏ2⟨k′|V^|ψ(+)⟩

To first order in V^, replace |ψ(+)⟩→|k⟩:

fB(k′,k)=−m2πℏ2⟨k′|V^|k⟩=−m2πℏ2V~(k′−k)

where V~(q)=∫d3re−iq⋅rV(r) is the Fourier transform of the potential.

Propagating Wave Packets ​

Real scattering experiments involve localized wave packets, not plane waves. A wave packet at t=0:

ψ(r,0)=∫d3k(2π)3/2ϕ(k)eik⋅r

evolves as:

ψ(r,t)=∫d3k(2π)3/2ϕ(k)eik⋅r−iE(k)t/ℏ

For a narrow momentum distribution around k0, expand E(k)≈E(k0)+ℏvg⋅(k−k0) with group velocity vg=∇kE/ℏ. The wave packet propagates without distortion (in free space) at velocity vg:

ψ(r,t)≈e−iE(k0)t/ℏψ(r−vgt,0)

Under scattering, the outgoing wave packet is a superposition of scattered spherical waves from each momentum component, leading to the differential cross section.

Part IV: Linear Response Theory ​

The Setting: Systems in Thermal Equilibrium ​

Linear response theory addresses a fundamental question: How does a macroscopic quantum system respond to weak external probes?

Consider a system described by H^0 at temperature T, with equilibrium density matrix:

ρ^0=e−βH^0Z,Z=Tr(e−βH^0)

where β=1/(kBT). The expectation value of any observable B^ in equilibrium is:

⟨B^⟩0=Tr(ρ^0B^)

At t=t0, we turn on a weak perturbation coupled to some operator A^:

V^(t)=−A^F(t)θ(t−t0)

where F(t) is a classical external field. We wish to compute the induced change in ⟨B^⟩.

Single-Particle vs. Many-Particle Settings ​

Single-particle quantum mechanics: The system is in a pure state |ψ(t)⟩. Expectation values are ⟨B^⟩=⟨ψ(t)|B^|ψ(t)⟩. The density matrix is ρ^=|ψ⟩⟨ψ| (a projector).

Statistical mechanics/condensed matter: The system is an ensemble described by ρ^. For N∼1023 particles, pure states are intractable. We work with:

  • Canonical ensemble: ρ^=e−βH^/Z
  • Grand canonical ensemble: ρ^=e−β(H^−μN^)/Z

The unified language is the density matrix formalism, where ⟨B^⟩=Tr(ρ^B^) reduces to the quantum average for pure states (ρ^2=ρ^) and statistical average for mixed states.

Density Matrix Evolution ​

In the interaction picture, the density matrix evolves as:

ρ^I(t)=U^(t,t0)ρ^0U^†(t,t0)

To first order in V^:

ρ^I(t)≈ρ^0+1iℏ∫t0t[V^I(t′),ρ^0]dt′

Deriving the Kubo Formula ​

The change in ⟨B^⟩ is:

δ⟨B^(t)⟩=Tr[ρ^I(t)B^I(t)]−Tr[ρ^0B^I(t)]

Using the cyclic property of trace and noting ρ^0 commutes with H^0 (hence [ρ^0,B^I(t)]≠0 in general, but ρ^0 is time-independent):

δ⟨B^(t)⟩=1iℏ∫t0tTr{[V^I(t′),ρ^0]B^I(t)}dt′

With V^I(t′)=−A^I(t′)F(t′) and using Tr([X^,Y^]Z^)=Tr(X^[Y^,Z^]):

δ⟨B^(t)⟩=1iℏ∫t0tF(t′)Tr{ρ^0[A^I(t′),B^I(t)]}dt′

Recognizing Tr(ρ^0[⋅,⋅])=⟨[⋅,⋅]⟩0:

δ⟨B^(t)⟩=∫t0tχBA(t−t′)F(t′)dt′

where the retarded response function (Kubo formula) is:

χBA(t)=−iℏθ(t)⟨[B^I(t),A^I(0)]⟩0

Key features:

  1. Causality: The θ(t) ensures response follows stimulus
  2. Equilibrium correlation: The commutator is evaluated in the unperturbed thermal state
  3. Quantum structure: The commutator encodes both classical and quantum fluctuations

Frequency Domain and Spectral Representation ​

For harmonic driving F(t)=F0e−iωt+ϵt (adiabatic turn-on), the response is:

δ⟨B^(ω)⟩=χBA(ω)F(ω)

with the susceptibility:

χBA(ω)=∫0∞eiωtχBA(t)dt

Insert a complete set of eigenstates |n⟩ of H^0 with energies En:

χBA(ω)=1Z∑n,me−βEn(⟨n|B^|m⟩⟨m|A^|n⟩ℏω+En−Em+iϵ−⟨n|A^|m⟩⟨m|B^|n⟩ℏω+Em−En+iϵ)

This spectral representation reveals the poles at excitation energies ℏω=Em−En.

Fluctuation-Dissipation Theorem ​

Consider the symmetrized correlation function:

SBA(ω)=∫−∞∞eiωt12⟨{B^I(t),A^I(0)}⟩0dt

and the response function's imaginary part χBA″(ω)=ImχBA(ω). Using the spectral representation and detailed balance e−βEn−e−βEm=(1−e−βℏω)e−βEn for ω=(Em−En)/ℏ:

χAA″(ω)=12ℏ(1−e−βℏω)SAA(ω)

Physical interpretation: The imaginary part of the susceptibility (dissipation) is proportional to the power spectrum of equilibrium fluctuations. At high temperatures (βℏω≪1), this reduces to the classical form χ″=(ω/2kBT)S.

Connection to Single-Particle Formulas ​

For a pure state at T=0, the density matrix is ρ^0=|ψ0⟩⟨ψ0| where |ψ0⟩ is the ground state. The Kubo formula reduces to:

χBA(t)=−iℏθ(t)⟨ψ0|[B^I(t),A^I(0)]|ψ0⟩

For a single-particle system, insert complete set of excited states:

χBA(t)=−iℏθ(t)∑n≠0(eiωn0t⟨0|B^|n⟩⟨n|A^|0⟩−e−iωn0t⟨0|A^|n⟩⟨n|B^|0⟩)

This matches the time-dependent perturbation theory result for transition amplitudes—the same physics expressed in the language of response functions. The many-body generalization simply replaces the ground state expectation with a thermal average, accounting for temperature and interactions.

Conductivity: The Kubo-Greenwood Formula ​

For electrical conductivity, the perturbation is V^=−er^⋅E(t) and the response current is j^. The current-current response gives:

σαβ(ω)=1ℏω∫0∞eiωt⟨[j^α(t),j^β(0)]⟩0dt

At T=0 for non-interacting electrons, this reduces to the Greenwood formula:

σαβ(ω)=πe2ℏ∑n,m(fn−fm)⟨n|v^α|m⟩⟨m|v^β|n⟩δ(Em−En−ℏω)

where fn are Fermi occupation factors. The connection is clear: conductivity measures the ease of inducing transitions (current) by an electric field, directly related to the density of states and transition matrix elements.

Summary and Connections ​

DomainInitial StatePerturbationKey QuantityPhysical Content
Atomic transitions|i⟩ (discrete)V^e−iωtWi→fTransition rate to continuum
Scattering|k⟩ (plane wave)Static V^f(k′,k)Differential cross section
Single-particle response|ψ0⟩ (pure state)−A^F(t)χBA(t)Ground state correlations
Many-body responseρ^0=e−βH^0/Z−A^F(t)χBA(ω)Thermal/fluctuation properties

The unifying framework is time-dependent perturbation theory in the interaction picture. The differences lie in:

  1. Initial conditions: Pure states vs. thermal ensembles
  2. Observables: Transition probabilities vs. induced expectation values
  3. Time domains: Transient evolution vs. steady-state response

The Kubo formula generalizes single-particle transition amplitudes to correlated many-body systems through the density matrix formalism.

References ​

  1. Sakurai, J.J. & Napolitano, J. Modern Quantum Mechanics, 3rd ed. (Cambridge University Press, 2020). Chapter 2, 5

  2. Taylor, J.R. Scattering Theory: The Quantum Theory of Nonrelativistic Collisions (Dover, 2006). Chapters 2–3, 9–10

  3. Mahan, G.D. Many-Particle Physics, 3rd ed. (Springer, 2000). Chapter 3 (Linear Response)

  4. Kubo, R. Statistical-Mechanical Theory of Irreversible Processes. I. J. Phys. Soc. Jpn. 12, 570 (1957)

  5. Fetter, A.L. & Walecka, J.D. Quantum Theory of Many-Particle Systems (Dover, 2003). Chapter 6 (Linear Response)
